description: Citrus, vinegar, and many fermented foods are 'tuko'. The adjective names acidic taste, distinct from 'puko' (bitter), and a food can carry both qualities. Sourness alone does not establish spoilage; 'mukesi' (spoil) names that deterioration.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip against the back of the upper teeth for voiceless dental 't' and release into high-back rounded 'u'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for 'k', then release into rounded true-mid back 'o'. Stress falls on 'tu'.
usage_notes: The adjective applies to taste rather than to a person's mood or manner. Its quality noun supplies sourness. Because 'tuko' and 'puko' differ only at the opening consonant, careful speech keeps the dental contact of 't' distinct from the lip closure of 'p'.

semantic_domains.aesthetic: an acidic taste encountered in food and drink
semantic_domains.physical: gustatory acidity such as citrus or vinegar
